title: Introduction to Robotics: A Social Work Perspective
description: Learn robotics fundamentals through real-world social service applications. Navigate ethical implementation decisions as you explore how robots are transforming social work practice with vulnerable populations.

nodes.title: Robotics Meets Social Practice
nodes.content: Social workers increasingly encounter robotic technology in practice settings. From companion robots that reduce isolation in elderly clients to mobility assistants that enhance independence for people with disabilities, robots are changing how we deliver services. Understanding robotics basics—how robots perceive their environment, make decisions, and interact with people—is essential for ethical, effective practice. This course explores robotics through scenarios you might face in social work, helping you make informed decisions about technology adoption.

nodes.title: Choosing Your Robotics Implementation Path
nodes.content: As a social work leader, you're evaluating how to introduce robotics into your agency. You've identified two potential focuses. Which aligns better with your agency's mission right now?

nodes.title: Direct Client Service with Robots
nodes.content: Client-facing robots provide direct assistance to vulnerable populations—companion robots reduce isolation in elderly clients, while mobility assistants enhance independence for people with disabilities. These robots combine sensors (cameras, touch sensors, pressure sensors) that perceive needs, actuators (motors) that enable movement, and algorithms that interpret sensor data and make decisions. A mobility robot's touch sensor detects when a client grabs it, its pressure sensor measures force, and its decision-making system determines whether lifting assistance is needed. Implementing client-facing robots requires understanding both technical capabilities and the ethical responsibilities of placing autonomous agents in intimate caregiving relationships.

nodes.title: Case Study: Maria's Companion Robot
nodes.content: Maria, 82, lives alone and experienced isolation after mobility decline. Your agency introduced a companion robot with cameras (sensors), microphones (sensors), speakers (actuators for voice), and wheels (actuators for movement). The robot's algorithms detect loneliness patterns and initiate engagement; when Maria says 'I'm thirsty,' its natural language processing alerts a caregiver. Crucially, Maria consented fully and retains control, keeping her dignity and autonomy central to this design.

nodes.title: How Will You Integrate Client-Facing Robots?
nodes.content: You've seen the potential of companion and mobility robots. Now you must decide how to deploy them in your agency. Two approaches are available, each with different implications for client outcomes, staff capacity, and ethical practice.

nodes.title: Ethical Implementation: A Success Story
nodes.content: Your agency took the gradual approach. You first trained all staff on robotics fundamentals: how sensors work, what algorithms can and cannot do, and how to recognize when a robot reaches its limitations. You developed detailed consent protocols ensuring every client understood the robot's capabilities, limitations, and data collection practices before use. You designated one skilled social worker as the 'robot liaison' to monitor client-robot interactions weekly and ensure relationships remained therapeutic. You preserved human judgment—robots provided recommendations, but humans made final decisions. You also built in regular audits: Does this robot actually reduce client isolation, or does it create pseudo-connection that undermines human relationships? Does it enhance independence or create technology dependence? One year later, 87% of clients reported increased well-being. Staff noted that robots handled routine tasks (medication reminders, low-stakes conversation), freeing social workers for deeper counseling and advocacy. Your agency demonstrated that robotics enhances social work; it doesn't replace it.

nodes.title: The Cost of Cutting Corners
nodes.content: Your agency deployed robots rapidly without adequate preparation. Staff received minimal training and didn't understand how sensors worked or what data robots collected. Clients were introduced with brief explanations: 'This robot will help you.' One elderly client felt watched and depersonalized by the camera and microphone. A younger client with arthritis asked the robot for help standing, but the robot misinterpreted his unclear request (sensor error) and applied incorrect force, injuring him. The agency faced liability claims and painful realization: they'd prioritized efficiency over dignity. Social workers felt overwhelmed troubleshooting failures and grew resentful rather than seeing robots as tools. Six months later, 70% of robots sat unused. The agency learned: robotics is a social innovation, not just technical. Client consent, staff competency, and ongoing monitoring are non-negotiable. This path shows why social workers must understand not just *what* robots can do, but *how* to ethically introduce them to vulnerable populations.

nodes.title: Organizational Robotics: Enabling Social Workers
nodes.content: Many agencies deploy 'back-office' robots to support operations rather than direct client care. Robotic process automation (RPA) uses software robots for repetitive tasks: scheduling, filing notes, sending reminders, and generating reports. Unlike physical robots, RPA robots interact purely with computer systems, following decision trees (if a client misses an appointment, robot checks availability, sends reminder, and escalates if needed). Ethical questions arise: data privacy is critical since robots access confidential information, and automation bias is a risk if social workers accept robot assessments without investigation.

nodes.title: Operational Excellence: Empowering Your Team
nodes.content: Your agency deployed organizational robotics successfully. RPA robots now handle 40% of administrative work—scheduling, appointment reminders, case note filing, and compliance reporting. Social workers reclaimed 8-10 hours per week for direct client care, supervision, and advocacy. You implemented robust safeguards: encrypted client data, regular audits of robot decision-making, and a policy that no client-affecting decision is made purely on robot recommendation. Staff meetings now focus on complex cases rather than coordination logistics. Client outcomes improved: longer counseling sessions, more community referrals, better follow-up on crises. Your agency demonstrated that robotics, when thoughtfully implemented, removes barriers to social work excellence. The robots don't practice social work—humans do. But humans now practice it better, unburdened by routine tasks. This approach shows how technology can support rather than replace the human heart of social work.
